critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split and explicitly acknowledges 'moderate autocorrelation of patient readmissions over time' while claiming this 'prioritizes detection of robust cross-sectional signals.' But the causal problem is not autocorrelation per se—it is that patients discharged later in the 2-year window may have been admitted under different protocols, with different comorbidity burdens, or during COVID-related hospital capacity changes. A random split means the model trains on future protocol contexts and tests on past ones (or vice versa), which does not reflect any deployment scenario. The justification for random splitting is that cross-sectional signals are 'robust,' but no analysis is presented to support this claim.

The RNN is applied to EHR records to capture 'sequence modeling capabilities pertinent to patient records.' Patient records are not a natural sequence in the same sense as time series data: each record is a bag of encounters, diagnoses, and medications, not a regularly sampled temporal signal. The sequence length varies enormously (a chronic patient has hundreds of encounters; a new patient has one). The design does not describe how variable-length sequences are handled (padding, truncation, attention masking), which affects whether the RNN is actually modeling temporal dynamics or simply learning from sequence length.

NLP-encoded discharge summaries are listed as features. Discharge summaries are written after the outcome has been partially known—the physician writing the summary may encode prognostic language that would not be available at the actual prediction time (prior to discharge). This is a form of label leakage through the feature, known as 'future knowledge leakage' or 'discharge summary leakage,' and is one of the most commonly cited methodological flaws in EHR-based readmission studies. The design does not address whether summaries are used in their final form or truncated to information available before discharge.

Both RNN and logistic regression are compared using AUROC, but the two models are expected to have very different calibration properties. AUROC measures discrimination without calibration; a well-calibrated model that assigns probabilities correctly across the risk spectrum may have lower AUROC than a poorly calibrated model with higher discrimination. For clinical decision-making (identifying patients who need interventions), calibration is as important as discrimination. No calibration evaluation (Brier score, calibration curves) is described.

Cross-validation is mentioned alongside a held-out test set, but the relationship between them is not defined. If cross-validation is used for hyperparameter tuning and the held-out test set for final evaluation, this is standard. But if cross-validation is applied to the full dataset (including the test set) and then the test set is also used for evaluation, this constitutes double-dipping. The description is ambiguous on this point.
all_issues_raised: Discharge summaries are written post-discharge and may encode prognostic language that was not available at prediction time; using final discharge summaries as features constitutes label leakage through a feature known as 'discharge summary leakage.'

Stratified random split across 2 years of hospital data ignores distributional shift from protocol changes, pandemic effects, and evolving patient populations; the 'cross-sectional signals are robust' claim is asserted without evidence.

Variable-length EHR sequences (from one encounter to hundreds) are handled by the RNN without describing padding, truncation, or attention masking, which determines whether the RNN is modeling temporal dynamics or simply learning from sequence length as a proxy for chronic disease burden.
AUROC-only evaluation ignores model calibration; for clinical intervention targeting, miscalibrated probability outputs undermine deployment utility regardless of discrimination performance.
The relationship between cross-validation and the held-out test set is ambiguous; if cross-validation spans the full dataset and the test set is evaluated separately, it is potentially double-dipped.
all_issues_adjudicated: Discharge summaries are written post-discharge and may encode prognostic language that was not available at prediction time; using final discharge summaries as features constitutes label leakage through a feature known as 'discharge summary leakage.'

Stratified random split across 2 years of hospital data ignores distributional shift from protocol changes, pandemic effects, and evolving patient populations; the 'cross-sectional signals are robust' claim is asserted without evidence.

Variable-length EHR sequences (from one encounter to hundreds) are handled by the RNN without describing padding, truncation, or attention masking, which determines whether the RNN is modeling temporal dynamics or simply learning from sequence length as a proxy for chronic disease burden.
AUROC-only evaluation ignores model calibration; for clinical intervention targeting, miscalibrated probability outputs undermine deployment utility regardless of discrimination performance.
The relationship between cross-validation and the held-out test set is ambiguous; if cross-validation spans the full dataset and the test set is evaluated separately, it is potentially double-dipped.
